mappings.rationaleAI: Philosophical theories of beauty from Plato through Keats address whether beauty is inherent or subjective, mapping directly to diverse theories concerning the nature of art (A.2) and fundamental conceptions of art's scope (A).

The Muses' patronage of poetry, song, music, and dance raises foundational questions about whether artistic inspiration is divine gift or cultivated power, directly addressing conceptions of art's scope (A.1) and the creative process (A.3).

Examination of whether exceptional creative ability is innate or cultivated, from divine inspiration in antiquity through Romantic conceptions of the solitary creator, directly addresses the creative process (A.3) and conceptions of art's scope (A.1).

Benjamin's argument that mechanical reproduction dissolved art's traditional aura and transformed its aesthetic experience and political function is a landmark contribution to theories of art's nature and effects (A.2), while his focus on photography and film addresses the production of artistic works as physical objects through new technologies (B.1.b).

Critical theory's argument that mass culture (film, radio, popular music) functions to maintain capitalist social conformity constitutes a pragmatic theory of art's effects (A.2) and addresses the characterization of art by social circumstances and audience, including the popular arts (B.3).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Avant Garde's Decline and Fall in the 20th Century
mappings.rationaleAI: The social function of art, exhaustion of progressive artistic narratives from Dadaism through Pop Art, and tension between aesthetic radicalism and institutional absorption address conceptions of art's scope (A.1), theories of art's functions (A.2), and the nature and varieties of style (B.4).

Tolstoy's renunciation of fiction in favour of moral truth and his conviction that art serves no purpose unless it advances ethical commitment exemplify the tension between aesthetic achievement and pragmatic theories of art's function (A.2) and competing conceptions of art's scope (A.1).

Morris's Arts and Crafts movement sought to reunite beauty with daily life and diagnosed the disconnection between art and society, addressing pragmatic theories of art's functions (A.2), conceptions of art's scope (A.1), and the distinction between useful and fine art (B.1.a).

The Secession's declaration that no distinction exists between fine and applied art directly challenges the useful/fine art classification (B.1.a), while its break from academic convention exemplifies period and school style formation (B.4.b) and redefines conceptions of art's scope (A.1).

The Bauhaus combined arts and crafts education, erasing boundaries between fine art, design, and architecture, directly addressing the classification of useful and fine art (B.1.a) and establishing a defining school style of twentieth-century modernism (B.4.b).

Photography's emergence provoked new theories of visual representation and debate over whether it killed painting or spurred artistic reinvention, addressing the manipulation of physical matter in art production (B.1.b), distinctions among kinds of art (B.2), and theories of art's nature (A.2).

The waltz's evolution from social dance to high art across music, ballet, poetry, and modernist reinterpretation illustrates performers as interpreters and creators (B.1.c) and the interrelation of time arts and space arts (B.2).

Hokusai's ukiyo-e woodblock printing and incorporation of Western pigments into Japanese printmaking address the production of artistic works as physical objects (B.1.b), cultural circumstances of art production (B.3), and ethnic and personal style (B.4.b).

The Gothic tradition spanning literature, architecture, and visual arts from Walpole through the Victorian era exemplifies a major period style (B.4.b), while its embrace of the irrational and sublime as an anti-Enlightenment force constitutes a distinct expressive theory of art (A.2).

Discussion of formalist, historicist, postcolonial, and theoretical approaches to Shakespeare surveys diverse critical theories of art (A.2), while debates over canonicity and cultural capital address characterization of works by cultural and social circumstances (B.3).

Bohemianism as a philosophy of life lived for art, connecting Romantic and Modernist artistic communities across Paris and Britain, addresses the social and cultural circumstances of artistic production (B.3) and competing conceptions of what art encompasses (A.1).
Surrealism as an expressive theory of art emphasizing the unconscious (A.2), performers as creators of spontaneous works (B.1.c), and Surrealism as a distinctive period and school style (B.4.b).
